common_name: Mexican Yellow Water Lily (Yellow Water Lily)

planting.note: Plant tubers horizontally 2–4 inches deep in heavy aquatic planting mix in containers submerged 6–18 inches below the water surface; Mexican yellow water lily spreads vigorously by stolons and can naturalize in shallow ponds and slow-moving water throughout zones 8–11.

directions: Plant tubers in heavy aquatic soil (NOT potting mix — which floats) in containers submerged 6–18 inches below the water surface in full sun; Mexican yellow water lily is a native aquatic perennial of ponds, marshes, and slow-moving waters of the Gulf Coast region from Texas to Florida and into Mexico, producing the classic water lily form — floating, round pads and fragrant, multi-petaled flowers — but in a soft, clear yellow color that distinguishes it from most cultivated water lilies. It is notably more heat-tolerant and better adapted to warm, humid climates than the European water lily (N. alba) or most cultivated tropical water lily hybrids. The cheerful yellow flowers open in the morning and close by afternoon. This species spreads readily by horizontal rhizomes (stolons) to colonize pond edges in warm climates — beneficial in a natural pond setting, manageable in a contained water garden. An excellent choice for water gardens in zone 9a.

planting.note: Float plants on still to slow-moving water surfaces in a pond, tub garden, or container water garden; American frogbit spreads rapidly by runners and can cover a water surface in warm weather. Native throughout the eastern US, it is non-invasive in its natural range.

directions: Float plants on the surface of still to slow-moving water in partial shade to full sun; American frogbit is a native floating aquatic that forms dense mats of rounded, slightly spongy, heart-shaped leaves on still pond surfaces and sluggish backwaters across the eastern US from the Gulf to the Great Lakes. The small, three-petaled white flowers with yellow centers bob at the water surface in summer. The plant provides critical nursery habitat for fish fry, frogs, and aquatic invertebrates under its floating mat, creating a shelter zone that significantly increases pond biodiversity. As a floating plant with dangling roots that absorb nutrients directly from the water, it is highly effective for improving water clarity and reducing algae by competing for dissolved nutrients. Unlike the invasive European frogbit (Hydrocharis morsus-ranae), American frogbit is native to the region and appropriate for naturalistic ponds and water gardens in the eastern US.

planting.note: Anchor bunches loosely in pond substrate or simply allow to float freely; hornwort requires no soil — it absorbs all nutrients directly from the water column. It is one of the most effective submerged oxygenating plants for backyard ponds in zones 4–10.

directions: Place bunches in the pond, anchored loosely in gravel or floating freely; hornwort is one of the most widely distributed native aquatic plants in North America — a rootless, submerged plant with whorled, needle-like leaves arranged in dense clusters that give the underwater stems the appearance of a raccoon's tail (hence 'coontail'). As a highly efficient oxygenator, it absorbs carbon dioxide and releases oxygen into the water column, improving water quality and supporting fish and aquatic invertebrate populations. It also absorbs dissolved nutrients (nitrogen, phosphorus) directly from the water, competing powerfully with algae for resources and contributing to pond clarity. Unlike most aquatic plants, it requires no soil and can be grown anchored in gravel or floating freely. In winter in the north, it sinks to the pond bottom and re-emerges in spring. Widely used in fish tanks and garden ponds for its efficiency and ease of care.

planting.note: Plant rooted divisions in moist to wet, fertile aquatic soil at pond margins or in very shallow water (0–3 inches deep); kidneyleaf mudplantain grows naturally in shallow, slow-moving water, pond margins, and muddy shores throughout the eastern US and is excellent for transitional water-to-land margins.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun at pond margins, in emergent zones, and in muddy shores; kidneyleaf mudplantain is a small native aquatic wildflower of still and slow-moving shallow water across the eastern US and tropical America, producing tiny clusters of delicate white and yellow flowers that provide nectar for small native bees and insects at the water margin. The kidney-shaped to oval floating leaves are bright, glossy green. The plant grows in the emergent zone — in muddy shores and water up to 3 inches deep — creating a low, creeping mat that stabilizes muddy pond edges and transitional zones. It is one of the most inconspicuous but ecologically important native aquatics, providing shallow-water cover for small fish, tadpoles, and aquatic invertebrates. Excellent for naturalizing the transitional zone between water and shore in native pond gardens.

planting.note: Anchor bunches in pond substrate or allow to float freely; common waterweed is a native, non-invasive oxygenating plant for backyard ponds, water gardens, and natural ponds in zones 4–10. It prefers cooler water temperatures than the invasive Brazilian elodea (Egeria densa).

directions: Anchor bunches in pond substrate or allow to root naturally in the pond bottom in partial shade to full sun; common waterweed is the native North American alternative to the widely used but invasive Brazilian elodea (Egeria densa), providing the same critical oxygenating and water-purifying services in backyard ponds without the risk of invasion in regional waterways. The whorled, small, lance-shaped leaves are arranged in tight spirals along the stems, and tiny, three-petaled white flowers float at the water surface on delicate threads in summer. As a highly efficient oxygenator, it absorbs CO₂ and releases O₂ through photosynthesis, supporting fish and aquatic life. It also absorbs dissolved nutrients, competing with algae for phosphorus and nitrogen. Prefers cool to moderate water temperatures and performs best in ponds with good water movement. The stems are consumed by waterfowl, turtles, and aquatic invertebrates.
